title: Project Freedom: 1,500 Ships Stuck, 2 Cleared, One 'Dome'
content:
## Project Freedom: 1,500 Ships Stuck, 2 Cleared, One 'Dome'

The administration says the lane is open. The evidence says otherwise.

"Project Freedom" is what the Pentagon calls its effort to clear a safe shipping corridor through the Strait of Hormuz. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth described the US military presence as a "red, white and blue dome" protecting commerce. Joint Chiefs Chairman Gen. Dan Caine said the lane is clear and that attacks on shipping "do not meet the threshold" of violating the ceasefire — a neat piece of linguistic engineering that reclassifies ongoing attacks as non-violations.

On the first day the lane was supposedly cleared, two US-flag ships made it through.

Approximately 1,500 ships remain stuck in the Persian Gulf. About 1,000 of those are large ocean-going vessels that cannot move. Iran has publicly stated it still controls the strait and that non-approved routes will face "decisive response."

European allies — the UK and France — have declined to participate until there is a "permanent ceasefire." That is an open-ended condition Iran controls. So the coalition of the willing consists of the US, and the lane is open in the sense that the US says it is.

Brent crude is at $109 a barrel. That's up 44% since the war began. Oil prices gained the same week Trump's rhetoric — "dented hopes" of a Hormuz deal, per reporting — suggested the strait wasn't functioning as a transit route.

**The numbers:**
- Ships cleared on Day 1 of "Project Freedom": 2
- Ships still stuck in the Persian Gulf: ~1,500
- Official position: the lane is open
- Physical reality: 1,500 to 2

Redefining what counts as a ceasefire violation doesn't move the ships. A "dome" that doesn't cover the waterway doesn't protect the sailors. The gap between the claim and what's actually happening in the water is the story.
